explanation: The `/status` command shows session information. Output includes: - Model being used - Context usage percentage - Session cost - Token counts Example output: `Model: Sonnet | Ctx: 45.2k | Cost: $1.23 | Ctx(u): 42.0%` Use `/stats` for usage statistics with activity graphs.

question: What flag limits maximum API spend in headless mode?

options.b: --max-budget-usd
options.c: --spending-cap

explanation: The `--max-budget-usd` flag sets maximum API spend (only with `--print`): ```bash claude -p "analyze" --max-budget-usd 5.00 ``` This prevents runaway costs in automated pipelines. The operation stops if the budget is exceeded.

question: How do you run a shell command directly from Claude Code prompt?

options.b: !command
options.c: $command

explanation: The `!command` prefix runs a shell command directly. Quick actions: - `!command` - Run shell command - `@filename` - Reference file - `Ctrl+C` - Cancel operation - `Ctrl+R` - Retry last Example: `!git status` runs git status without Claude interpreting it.

question: What is the correct way to resume a specific session by ID?

options.b: claude -r abc123
options.c: claude --load abc123

explanation: Use `-r` or `--resume` to resume a specific session: ```bash claude -r abc123 # Resume session abc123 claude --resume abc123 # Same as above claude -c # Resume last session (short) claude --continue # Resume last session (long) ``` Combine with `-p` for scripting: `claude -r abc123 -p "check status"`

question: What is the recommended .gitignore pattern for Claude Code files?

options.b: Ignore settings.local.json and .claude/CLAUDE.md, keep agents/commands/hooks
options.c: Don't ignore anything

explanation: Recommended .gitignore: ```gitignore # Claude Code - Personal (ignore) .claude/settings.local.json .claude/CLAUDE.md .claude/.serena/ # Claude Code - Team (keep/commit) # .claude/agents/ # .claude/commands/ # .claude/hooks/ # .claude/settings.json ``` This keeps team workflows shared while personal settings stay private.

question: What is the daily workflow morning setup according to the guide?

options.b: Git pull, /status, load project memory, review yesterday's progress
options.c: Run all tests first

explanation: Morning setup workflow: 1. Git pull latest changes 2. Review context with `/status` 3. Load project memory (`/sc:load` if using Serena) 4. Review yesterday's progress This ensures you start with fresh code and full context awareness.

question: What flag allows Claude's tools to access directories outside the current working directory?

options.b: --add-dir
options.c: --context-dir

explanation: Use `--add-dir` to allow tool access to additional directories: ```bash claude --add-dir ../shared ../utils claude --add-dir packages/api ``` By default, Claude can only access files in the current working directory. Use --add-dir to extend permissions to other directories (e.g., shared libraries in a monorepo).

question: When Sequential Thinking MCP seems slow or unresponsive, what should you expect?

options.b: 10-30 second responses are normal due to significant compute
options.c: Switch to a different MCP

explanation: Sequential Thinking uses significant compute - expect 10-30 second responses. This is not an error, just be patient. Tips for Sequential: - Works best with specific, well-defined problems - Good: "Debug why user authentication fails on mobile" - Bad: "Make the app better" The longer response time reflects deeper analysis.

question: What shortcut opens an external editor for composing long text input?

options.b: Ctrl+G
options.c: Ctrl+O

explanation: `Ctrl+G` opens an external editor for composing long text. Useful input shortcuts: - `Ctrl+A`: Jump to beginning of line - `Ctrl+E`: Jump to end of line - `Ctrl+W`: Delete previous word - `Ctrl+G`: Open external editor - `Tab`: Autocomplete file paths The external editor allows comfortable editing of complex prompts.

question: What does the '--from-pr' flag do in Claude Code v2.1.27?

options.b: Resumes sessions linked to a GitHub PR number or URL
options.c: Fetches all PR comments into context

explanation: The `--from-pr` flag (v2.1.27) lets you resume Claude Code sessions linked to a specific GitHub PR. Usage: `claude --from-pr 123` or `claude --from-pr https://github.com/org/repo/pull/123`. This loads the PR context (diff, comments, checks) directly into the session.

question: What breaking change was introduced in Claude Code v2.1.19 for hook/command arguments?

options.b: Migration from $ARGUMENTS.0 (dot syntax) to $ARGUMENTS[0] (bracket syntax)
options.c: New $PARAMS variable replaced $ARGUMENTS

explanation: Claude Code v2.1.19 changed argument access syntax from dot notation (`$ARGUMENTS.0`) to bracket notation (`$ARGUMENTS[0]`). This breaking change affects all custom commands and hooks that reference arguments. Update your scripts to use the new bracket syntax.

question: According to the guide's 'Myths vs Reality' section, what is the truth about 'hidden Claude Code features'?

options.a: Secret flags exist for power users
options.b: Hidden features require an enterprise plan
options.c: There are no hidden features - Anthropic uses progressive rollout, not secret flags

explanation: The Myths vs Reality section debunks the "hidden features" myth. Anthropic uses progressive rollout (features gradually enabled for users) rather than secret flags or hidden configurations. All features are documented in release notes. There is no secret power-user mode.

question: What are the three developer patterns when using AI, according to the Learning with AI guide?

options.b: Dependent, Avoidant, Augmented
options.c: Passive, Active, Proactive

explanation: The three patterns are: Dependent (copy-paste without understanding, can't debug AI code), Avoidant (refuses AI on principle, slower than peers), and Augmented (uses AI critically, understands everything). The goal is to become Augmented - using AI for leverage while maintaining deep understanding.

question: What does UVAL stand for in the AI learning protocol?

options.b: Understand, Verify, Apply, Learn
options.c: Understand, Validate, Analyze, Learn

explanation: UVAL stands for: Understand First (the 15-minute rule), Verify (ensure you actually learned), Apply (transform knowledge into skill through modification), Learn (capture insights for long-term retention). This protocol ensures you're learning, not just copying.

question: In the Quick Self-Check, if you score 0-2 'yes' answers, what does this indicate?

options.b: You're on track with room for optimization
options.c: You're at dependency risk - outsourcing thinking

explanation: A score of 0-2 yes answers indicates dependency risk - you're outsourcing your thinking to AI. The 5 questions test: can you explain AI-generated code, have you debugged without AI, do you know WHY solutions work, could you write the code yourself, do you know AI's limitations. Low scores mean you should read the "Breaking Dependency" section.

question: What is the '15-Minute Rule' in the UVAL protocol's 'Understand First' step?

options.a: Limit AI usage to 15 minutes per day
options.b: A 4-step protocol: State problem, Brainstorm approaches, Identify gaps, THEN ask AI
options.c: Wait 15 minutes after getting AI response before using it
options.d: Spend 15 minutes explaining AI code to a colleague

explanation: The 15-minute rule is a specific protocol: (1) State the problem in ONE sentence (2 min), (2) Brainstorm 3 possible approaches (5 min), (3) Identify your knowledge gaps (3 min), (4) THEN ask AI with a much better question (5 min). This forces you to think before asking, resulting in better questions and faster learning.

question: What is the 'Explain It Back' technique in the UVAL protocol's Verify step?

options.a: Ask AI to explain its own code
options.b: Write documentation for the code
options.c: If you can't explain the code to a colleague, you haven't learned it
options.d: Record yourself explaining and review later

explanation: The rule is simple: if you can't explain the code to a colleague, you don't understand it. This is the verification step - you must be able to articulate WHY the solution works, not just THAT it works. The guide recommends using a /explain-back slash command that asks YOU to explain AI-generated code.

question: In the 30-Day Progression Plan, what is the AI usage ratio for Week 1?

options.b: 40-50% AI usage
options.c: 60-70% AI usage

explanation: Week 1 focuses on foundations with 0-20% AI usage: Days 1-2 build feature WITHOUT AI (0%), Days 4-5 refactor with AI review only (20%), Day 6 debug without AI (0%). The goal is to build (or rebuild) core skills without heavy AI reliance. Success criteria: can explain every line you wrote.

question: What AI usage ratio does the 30-Day Plan recommend for Week 4 (Augmented stage)?
explanation: Week 4 (Augmented stage) recommends 70% AI usage with the UVAL protocol. The progression is: Week 1 (0-20%), Week 2 (30-40%), Week 3 (50-60%), Week 4 (70%). Success criteria for Week 4: you're fast AND you understand everything. The cap at 70% ensures you maintain skills.

question: According to the guide, what is the metaphor for AI in development?

options.b: AI is your GPS
options.c: AI is your assistant

explanation: AI is compared to GPS: great for getting somewhere fast, dangerous if you lose the ability to navigate without it, truly useful when you understand the map AND use the GPS. A developer who only copy-pastes AI output is like a driver who can't read a map - fine until the GPS fails or someone asks for directions.

question: What is a 'Red Flag' sign of AI dependency according to the checklist?

options.b: Can't start coding without AI
options.c: Using AI for code reviews

explanation: "Can't start without AI" is a red flag indicating you've outsourced problem decomposition. Other red flags: don't understand AI's code, can't debug AI errors, anxiety without AI, rejected in interviews, always ask "how" never "why", every solution looks the same. Immediate action: code 30 min daily without AI.

question: What is the 'Apply' step's core principle in the UVAL protocol?

options.a: Apply the code directly to production
options.b: Transform knowledge into skill through MODIFICATION, not copying
options.c: Apply code reviews to all AI-generated code
options.d: Apply unit tests to verify correctness

explanation: The Apply step requires modification, not copy-paste. You must change at least one element (variable name, structure, edge case handling). Why? Modification forces understanding. Copying is passive; modifying requires you to engage with the code and understand how it works.

question: What CLAUDE.md configuration enables 'Learning Mode' with Claude Code?

options.a: ## Learning Mode - Ask me questions before generating code
options.b: ## Strict Mode - Never generate code directly
options.c: ## Teaching Mode - Always explain before showing

explanation: Learning Mode in CLAUDE.md prompts Claude to ask questions before generating: "What approaches have I considered?", "What specifically am I stuck on?", "What do I expect the solution to look like?". This implements the UVAL protocol's Understand step directly in your workflow.

question: What hook event is recommended for capturing daily learning?

options.b: PostToolUse - after each edit
options.c: Stop - when session ends

explanation: The learning-capture.sh hook uses the Stop event (session end) to prompt: "What's ONE thing you learned today?" This logs to ~/claude-learnings.md automatically. It's lightweight (asks one question) so you'll actually use it, unlike verbose learning journals.

question: What is the 'Dependent' pattern's main risk according to the guide?

options.b: Unemployable
options.c: Left behind

explanation: The Dependent pattern's risk is becoming unemployable. Signs: copy-paste without understanding, can't debug AI code, anxiety without AI. In interviews: can't whiteboard basics, struggles with "why this approach?", asks to look up fundamentals. You ship code you can't explain - when it breaks, you're stuck.

question: What is the Weekly Self-Audit question that detects dependency?

options.a: How many lines of code did I write?
options.b: Am I faster than last month? Am I smarter?
options.c: How much did I use AI this week?
options.d: Did I meet all my deadlines?

explanation: The key question is: "Am I faster than last month? Am I smarter?" If you're faster but NOT smarter, you're building dependency. Other weekly questions: What did I learn that I didn't know before? Could I have done this without AI? Did I understand everything I shipped?

question: What is the recommended approach for 'Avoidant' developers (Pattern 2)?

options.a: Continue avoiding AI to maintain purity
options.b: Start with AI review of YOUR code, not AI-generated code
options.c: Immediately switch to 100% AI usage
options.d: Wait until AI tools are more mature

explanation: For Avoidant developers, the guide recommends gradual adoption: start with AI reviewing YOUR code (you stay in control), then move to AI explaining concepts you implement, then AI-assisted work. This respects your instinct for understanding while gaining AI benefits. Pure avoidance means being slower without being smarter.

question: What are the 3 failure modes identified in Addy Osmani's '80% Problem' of agentic coding?

options.b: Overengineering, assumption propagation, sycophantic agreement
options.c: Slow responses, wrong language, missed tests

explanation: Addy Osmani's "80% Problem" identifies 3 failure modes: 1. **Overengineering** - AI adds unnecessary complexity (abstraction layers, premature optimization) 2. **Assumption propagation** - One wrong assumption cascades through the codebase 3. **Sycophantic agreement** - AI agrees with bad ideas instead of pushing back The 80% refers to AI getting you 80% of the way, with the last 20% requiring deep human understanding.

question: What are the 3 symptoms of vibe coding 'context overload'?

options.a: Memory leaks, slow startup, API rate limits
options.b: Big-bang context dumps, 5K+ line prompts, performance degradation
options.c: Lost files, broken imports, missing tests
options.d: Excessive commits, merge conflicts, branch proliferation

explanation: Context overload symptoms in vibe coding: 1. **Big-bang context dumps** - Pasting entire codebases into prompts 2. **5K+ line prompts** - Exceeding effective context window usage 3. **Performance degradation** - Claude's quality drops as context grows The fix: progressive disclosure (feed context incrementally), use CLAUDE.md for persistent context, and start fresh sessions between phases.

question: What are the two levels of Team-Level Steering Metrics for AI-augmented engineering?

options.a: Level 1 = individual productivity; Level 2 = team productivity
options.b: Level 1 = DORA delivery health (Deployment Frequency + Lead Time); Level 2 = AI adoption quality (CFR by code origin, review time comparison)
options.c: Level 1 = code quality; Level 2 = business outcomes
options.d: Level 1 = token usage; Level 2 = cost per feature

explanation: Team-Level Steering Metrics operate at two levels. Level 1 tracks DORA delivery health: Deployment Frequency and Lead Time for Changes, which measure whether AI is actually accelerating delivery. Level 2 goes deeper into AI adoption quality: Change Failure Rate broken down by code origin (AI-generated vs human-written), and review time comparison between the two. Level 2 metrics catch the velocity trap — teams that look fast on DORA but are accumulating hidden risk.

question: What is the velocity trap in AI-augmented teams, and why is DORA 'High' on deployment frequency not sufficient evidence of health?

options.a: Teams deploy too slowly because AI adds overhead
options.b: High deployment frequency can coexist with burnout, skill atrophy, and shallow reviews — DORA measures pace, not sustainability or quality
options.c: DORA metrics are only valid for teams of 10 or more engineers
options.d: Deployment frequency causes merge conflicts when AI generates too many branches

explanation: The velocity trap: a team rated High on DORA deployment frequency can simultaneously experience burnout (engineers rubber-stamping AI output), skill atrophy (engineers losing the ability to reason about code independently), and shallow reviews (speed pressure discourages deep inspection). DORA measures pace of delivery, but not whether the team understands what it is shipping. This is why Level 2 metrics (CFR by code origin, review time) exist — to surface quality degradation that hides behind fast numbers.

question: What is the '3 metrics, 5 minutes Monday ritual' for tech leads, and what does it protect against?

options.a: A daily standup format that replaces sprint retrospectives
options.b: A weekly check of deployment frequency, lead time, and test coverage to satisfy compliance requirements
options.c: A weekly review of three steering metrics in under five minutes to catch drift early, before velocity-trap patterns solidify into team habits
options.d: A tool that automatically generates weekly reports from git history

explanation: The Monday ritual is a lightweight weekly habit for tech leads: spend five minutes reviewing three steering metrics (typically Deployment Frequency, CFR by code origin, and review time delta). The goal is early detection of drift. Velocity traps, skill atrophy, and review shortcuts develop gradually — weekly check-ins catch the signal before it becomes a cultural pattern that is hard to reverse. Five minutes is intentionally short: the ritual should have near-zero friction so it actually happens.

question: What is the core architecture pattern of Claude Code?

options.a: A DAG orchestrator with task planning
options.b: A simple while(tool_call) loop with no classifier
options.c: A RAG pipeline with embeddings
options.d: A multi-agent system with intent router

explanation: Claude Code runs a remarkably simple `while(tool_call)` loop. There is NO intent classifier, task router, RAG/embedding pipeline, DAG orchestrator, or planner/executor split. The model itself decides when to call tools, which tools to call, and when it's done. This is the "agentic loop" pattern.

question: How many core tools does Claude Code have?

options.b: 8 tools
options.c: 12 tools

explanation: Claude Code has 8 core tools: Bash (universal adapter), Read (file contents), Edit (modify files), Write (create/overwrite), Grep (search contents), Glob (find files), Task (sub-agents), and the Tasks API for progress tracking. Note: TodoWrite was deprecated in v2.1.16+ and replaced by the Tasks API (TaskCreate, TaskGet, TaskList, TaskUpdate) which adds task dependencies via blockedBy/blocks fields.

question: Which tool is described as Claude's 'swiss-army knife' and 'universal adapter'?

options.b: Grep
options.c: Bash

explanation: Bash is Claude's swiss-army knife. It can run any CLI tool (git, npm, docker, curl...), execute scripts, chain commands with pipes, and access system state. The model has been trained on massive amounts of shell data, making it highly effective as a universal adapter.

question: What is the approximate context budget for Claude Code (e.g., with Sonnet 4.6)?

options.b: ~100K tokens
options.c: ~200K tokens

explanation: Claude Code operates within a ~200K token context window. This is shared between: system prompt (~5-15K), CLAUDE.md files (~1-10K), conversation history (variable), tool results (variable), and reserved response buffer (~40-45K). Usable space is approximately 140-150K tokens.

question: What are the reported auto-compaction thresholds for Claude Code?

options.b: 75-92% (conflicting reports)

explanation: Auto-compaction thresholds vary by source: PromptLayer analysis reports 92%, community observations report 75-80%. When triggered, older conversation turns are summarized, tool results condensed, and recent context preserved. Use /compact to manually trigger summarization.

question: What is the maximum depth for sub-agents spawned via the Task tool?

options.b: Depth = 3
options.c: Depth = 1 (cannot spawn sub-sub-agents)

explanation: Sub-agents have a depth=1 limit. They CANNOT spawn sub-sub-agents. This prevents: recursive explosion (infinite resources), context pollution (accumulated context), debugging nightmares (multi-level chains), and unpredictable costs (nested token usage).

question: What does a sub-agent receive when spawned by the Task tool?

options.a: Full conversation history + all file reads
options.b: Task description only (isolated fresh context)
options.c: Last 10 messages of conversation

explanation: Sub-agents have ISOLATED context. They receive only the task description, have their own fresh context window, access the same tools (except Task), and return only a summary text. This isolation keeps the main context clean and prevents context pollution.

question: What are the 4 permission layers in Claude Code's security model?

options.b: Interactive prompts, Allow/Deny rules, Hooks, Sandbox
options.c: Read, Write, Execute, Delete

explanation: Claude Code has 4 layered security: (1) Interactive prompts (allow once/always/deny), (2) Allow/Deny rules in settings.json, (3) Hooks (Pre/Post execution scripts), (4) Sandbox mode (filesystem + network isolation). Each layer adds protection.

question: What algorithm does the Edit tool use when exact match fails?

options.b: Fuzzy match (whitespace normalization, line ending normalization, context expansion)
options.c: Regex pattern matching

explanation: When exact match fails, Edit attempts fuzzy matching: (1) Whitespace normalization (trailing spaces, indentation), (2) Line ending normalization (CRLF vs LF), (3) Context expansion (surrounding lines). Only if fuzzy match also fails does it return an error.

question: What protocol does MCP (Model Context Protocol) use for communication?

options.b: GraphQL
options.c: JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio or HTTP

explanation: MCP uses JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio or HTTP transport. MCP tools follow the naming convention `mcp__<server>__<tool>`. Servers start on first use and stay alive during the session. They have the same permission system as native tools.

question: What is Claude Code's design philosophy, as stated by Anthropic?

options.a: More scaffolding, less model - build complex orchestration
options.b: Less scaffolding, more model - trust Claude's reasoning
options.c: Maximum control - explicit rules for every case

explanation: Claude Code's philosophy is "Less scaffolding, more model" - trust Claude's reasoning instead of building complex orchestration systems. This means: single model decides (no classifier/router), Grep+Glob (no RAG), simple while loop (no DAG), conversation as state (no state machines).

question: What are the 4 specialized sub-agent types available in Claude Code?

options.b: Explore, Plan, Bash, general-purpose
options.c: Junior, Senior, Expert, Architect

explanation: Claude Code offers 4 sub-agent types: Explore (codebase exploration, read-only tools), Plan (architecture planning, no Edit/Write), Bash (command execution, Bash only), and general-purpose (complex multi-step tasks, all tools). Each has different tool access.

question: What tool replaced TodoWrite for task management in Claude Code v2.1.16+?

options.b: Kanban tool
options.c: Tasks API (TaskCreate, TaskGet, TaskList, TaskUpdate)

explanation: The Tasks API (v2.1.16+) replaced TodoWrite with 4 tools: TaskCreate (create tasks with descriptions), TaskGet (fetch full task details), TaskList (list all tasks with status), TaskUpdate (update status, dependencies, metadata). Key improvement: task dependencies via blockedBy/blocks fields.

question: What is the API cost overhead of fetching full details for 50 tasks via Tasks API?

options.b: 25x overhead (25 API calls)
options.c: 51x overhead (1 TaskList + 50 TaskGet calls required)

explanation: Tasks API limitation: TaskList returns only summary fields (id, subject, status, owner, blockedBy). To get full details (description, metadata), you need individual TaskGet calls. For 50 tasks: 1 TaskList + 50 TaskGet = 51 API calls. This N+1 pattern is a known limitation compared to TodoWrite which returned everything in one call.

question: What is TeammateTool and what is its status?

options.b: Experimental multi-agent coordination (spawnTeam, discoverTeams, requestJoin, approveJoin) - unstable, no official support
options.c: Official Anthropic tool for team collaboration
options.d: Deprecated feature replaced by Tasks API

explanation: TeammateTool is an experimental multi-agent coordination tool with 4 operations: spawnTeam, discoverTeams, requestJoin, approveJoin. Status: unstable, not officially supported by Anthropic. It allows Claude instances to form teams and coordinate, but the API is subject to change without notice. Not recommended for production use.

question: What is 'Context Rot' and what makes it structural rather than incidental?

options.a: Gradual quality degradation caused by too many code comments in the context window
options.b: The n² attention cost: as context grows, each new token attends to all previous tokens, causing exponential slowdown and quality degradation regardless of content quality
options.c: Rot caused by leaving unused variables and dead code in the context
options.d: API rate limiting that causes degraded responses when context is large

explanation: Context Rot is structural, not incidental: transformer attention is O(n²) — each new token attends to all previous tokens. As context grows, the effective attention per token dilutes, early context gets underweighted, and recent tokens dominate disproportionately. This happens even with high-quality context content. Solutions: /compact (prune), context chaining (fresh session with curated summary), or JIT retrieval (never load; fetch on demand). You cannot fix structural rot by cleaning up your prompts.

question: What is the key difference between JIT (Just-In-Time) retrieval and RAG in the context of Claude Code sub-agents?

options.a: JIT retrieval uses vector databases while RAG uses keyword search
options.b: JIT retrieval never pre-loads context — sub-agents fetch only what they need at the moment of need; RAG pre-indexes and embeds documents upfront for semantic lookup
options.c: JIT is faster but less accurate; RAG is slower but more precise
options.d: JIT is for code retrieval only; RAG handles documentation retrieval

explanation: JIT retrieval in sub-agents means: never pre-load context — the agent identifies what it needs (WHY/WHAT pattern), fetches only that, processes it, then discards. This keeps context windows small per agent. RAG pre-indexes and embeds a corpus upfront, enabling semantic similarity lookup at query time. For Claude Code sub-agents: JIT is preferred for targeted retrieval tasks (no embedding setup, no stale index); RAG suits knowledge bases queried repeatedly across many sessions.

question: What is MMD (Maximum Mean Discrepancy) used for in context drift detection?

options.a: Measuring the maximum number of tokens that can drift before a session crashes
options.b: A statistical test comparing the distribution of recent token embeddings to a baseline distribution to detect semantic drift in ongoing conversations
options.c: Tracking the maximum memory distance between two related CLAUDE.md files
options.d: A compression algorithm that minimizes mean deviation in context summaries

explanation: MMD (Maximum Mean Discrepancy) is a kernel-based statistical test that compares two distributions without assuming a parametric form. In context drift detection: you embed tokens/messages from a baseline window and a recent window, then compute MMD between the two distributions. A high MMD score indicates the conversation has drifted semantically from its original topic — a signal to /compact or start a fresh session. Threshold: MMD > 0.3 typically warrants intervention. More robust than cosine distance for multi-modal drift.

question: What is the 'Goldilocks altitude problem' for CLAUDE.md project rules?

options.a: Finding the right file size for CLAUDE.md — not too large (context waste) nor too small (insufficient guidance)
options.b: The challenge of writing rules at the right abstraction level: too high-level (ignored/mis-applied) vs too low-level (brittle, over-specified)
options.c: Choosing the right directory depth for nested CLAUDE.md files in a monorepo
options.d: Balancing rule strictness — too strict causes Claude to refuse valid tasks, too loose provides no guidance

explanation: The Goldilocks altitude problem: rules written too high ("write good code") are too abstract to apply consistently. Rules written too low ("always add a blank line after function declarations") are brittle — they conflict with context or become stale. The sweet spot is mid-altitude: specific enough to be actionable, abstract enough to generalize across similar situations. Example of right altitude: "prefer functional style for data transformations; use classes only when you need stateful lifecycle management."

question: What are the recommended MCP server and tool count limits to avoid context overhead?

options.b: Max 10 servers, max 80 total tools across all servers
options.c: No limit — Claude handles any number of MCP tools efficiently

explanation: Recommended limits: fewer than 10 MCP servers and fewer than 80 total tools across all servers. Each MCP server's tools are announced at session start and consume context window space permanently for that session. Beyond ~80 tools, the tool announcement overhead meaningfully degrades available context for actual work. Beyond 10 servers, initialization latency and inter-server coordination complexity compound. Audit your MCP stack: disable unused servers in settings.json, use scoped configs per project type.

question: What is an 'MCP Rug Pull' attack?

options.a: An MCP server that crashes unexpectedly
options.b: A benign MCP that turns malicious after gaining trust (no re-approval needed)
options.c: An MCP that uses too many tokens
options.d: An attack on the MCP protocol itself

explanation: An MCP Rug Pull exploits the one-time approval model: attacker publishes benign MCP → user approves once → MCP works normally (builds trust) → attacker pushes malicious update → MCP exfiltrates credentials WITHOUT re-approval. Mitigation: version pinning + hash verification.

question: What does CVE-2025-53109/53110 (EscapeRoute) exploit?

options.a: Prompt injection in Claude's system prompt
options.b: Filesystem MCP sandbox escape via prefix bypass + symlinks
options.c: Memory corruption in the Bash tool
options.d: API key leakage in network requests

explanation: CVE-2025-53109/53110 (EscapeRoute) allows sandbox escape in Filesystem MCP via prefix bypass combined with symlinks. Severity: High. Mitigation: avoid Filesystem MCP with unrestricted access or apply the official patch. Source: Cymulate security research.

question: What is a known limitation of permissions.deny in .claude/settings.json?

options.b: System reminders may expose file contents before tool permission checks
options.c: It cannot block Bash commands

explanation: permissions.deny has architectural limitations: background indexing may expose file contents via internal "system reminder" mechanism BEFORE tool permission checks are applied. This is documented in GitHub #4160. Defense-in-depth: store secrets outside project directories.

question: What is the recommended defense-in-depth strategy for secrets protection?

options.b: Store secrets outside project + external vault + PreToolUse hooks + never commit
options.c: Encrypt all files in the project
options.d: Use a VPN when running Claude Code

explanation: Defense-in-depth: (1) Store secrets outside project directories (~/.secrets/ or vault), (2) Use external secrets management (AWS Secrets Manager, 1Password), (3) Add PreToolUse hooks as secondary blocking, (4) Never commit secrets, (5) Manually review bash commands.

question: Which prompt injection evasion technique uses U+200B, U+200C, U+200D?

options.b: RTL override
options.c: Zero-width characters (invisible to humans)

explanation: Zero-width characters (U+200B, U+200C, U+200D) make instructions invisible to humans while still being interpreted. Detection: Unicode regex pattern [\x{200B}-\x{200D}\x{FEFF}\x{202A}-\x{202E}]. Added to prompt-injection-detector.sh in v3.6.0.

question: Which secret detection tool has the highest recall (88%) but lower precision (46%)?

options.b: GitGuardian
options.c: Gitleaks

explanation: Gitleaks: 88% recall, 46% precision, fast (~2 min/100K commits) - best for pre-commit hooks. TruffleHog: 52% recall, 85% precision, slow - best for CI verification. GitGuardian: 80% recall, 95% precision - enterprise monitoring. detect-secrets: 60% recall, 98% precision - baseline approach.

question: What is the recommended hook stack for security in settings.json?

options.b: PreToolUse (dangerous blocker, injection detector) + PostToolUse (output scanner) + SessionStart (MCP integrity)
options.c: No hooks - rely only on permissions.deny

explanation: Recommended security hook stack: PreToolUse → dangerous-actions-blocker.sh (Bash), prompt-injection-detector.sh + unicode-injection-scanner.sh (Edit/Write). PostToolUse → output-secrets-scanner.sh (Bash). SessionStart → mcp-config-integrity.sh. Multiple layers for defense-in-depth.

question: Which MCP servers are marked as 'Safe' in the community-vetted safe list?

options.b: @anthropic/mcp-server-*, context7, sequential-thinking, memory
options.c: browser (full access), custom MCPs
options.d: All MCPs are safe by default

explanation: MCP Safe List: @anthropic/mcp-server-* (official), context7 (read-only docs), sequential-thinking (no external access, local), memory (local file-based). Risk: filesystem unrestricted (CVE-2025-53109), database prod (exfiltration). Unsafe: browser full access.

question: What is the first action when a secret is exposed?

options.b: Revoke the credential immediately
options.c: Scan the entire repo

explanation: First 15 minutes (stop the bleeding): (1) Revoke immediately - AWS delete-access-key, GitHub revoke token, Stripe roll key. (2) Confirm exposure scope. Then: audit git history, scan dependencies, check CI/CD logs. First 24 hours: rotate ALL related credentials, notify compliance, document timeline.

question: What are the three security posture levels in the guide?

options.b: Basic (5 min), Standard (30 min), Hardened (2 hours)
options.c: Development, Staging, Production

explanation: Security posture levels: Basic (5 min) = output scanner + dangerous blocker - for solo dev/experiments. Standard (30 min) = + injection hooks + MCP vetting - for teams/sensitive code. Hardened (2 hours) = + integrity verification + ZDR - for enterprise/production.

question: Which sandbox isolation approach combines microVM isolation with network policies for agent autonomy?

options.b: Fly.io Sprites (edge compute)
options.c: Docker Sandboxes (with custom templates and network policies)

explanation: Docker Sandboxes provide microVM-level isolation with customizable network policies. Key features: custom Dockerfile templates for reproducible environments, network policies to control egress/ingress, volume mounts for persistent storage, and CPU/memory limits. This approach suits teams wanting full control over sandbox configuration while maintaining strong isolation.

question: What is the GitHub Issue Auto-Creation Bug (#13797) and why is it dangerous?

options.b: Claude Code accidentally creates public GitHub issues containing private project details
options.c: Issues are created but remain private

explanation: The GitHub Issue Auto-Creation Bug (#13797) causes Claude Code to accidentally create public GitHub issues containing private project details. Over 17 accidental disclosures documented, affecting v2.0.65+. The danger: internal code, architecture decisions, and private context leak publicly. Mitigation: disable the gh CLI tool or restrict GitHub permissions.

question: Which paths are protected by the bypassPermissions safety invariant, meaning Claude ALWAYS prompts before modifying them?

options.b: .git/, .claude/, shell configs (.bashrc, .zshrc), .gitconfig, .mcp.json, and .claude.json
options.c: All dotfiles in the home directory

explanation: The bypassPermissions safety invariant protects a specific set of high-impact paths: .git/ (repository integrity), .claude/ (agent configuration, excluding .claude/worktrees/), shell config files (.bashrc, .zshrc — modifying these can persist malicious behavior across sessions), .gitconfig, .mcp.json, and .claude.json. Even when bypassPermissions is enabled, Claude always surfaces a confirmation prompt before touching these paths. The rationale: mistakes in these files have outsized, hard-to-reverse consequences.

question: What is the root cause of Cache Bug 2, and what is its cost impact on --resume sessions?

options.a: The API rate limiter resets token counters, causing full context re-reads on each resume
options.b: The JSONL writer strips deferred_tools_delta records before writing; on --resume, full re-announcement shifts all message positions, producing 0% cache hit rate and 87-118K tokens rebuilt per resume
options.c: Cache TTL expires after 5 minutes of inactivity, forcing a full context reload
options.d: Tool call results are not serialized, so they are regenerated from scratch on each resume

explanation: Cache Bug 2 root cause: the JSONL writer strips DTD (deferred_tools_delta) records before writing the conversation to disk. When a session is resumed with --resume, Claude performs a full re-announcement of all tools, which shifts the position of every subsequent message. Because Anthropic's prompt cache is position-sensitive, these shifted positions produce a 0% cache hit rate. Cost impact: 87,000 to 118,000 tokens are rebuilt from scratch on every --resume. Workaround: avoid using --resume for long sessions until the bug is fixed.

question: What does CLAUDE_CODE_ATTRIBUTION_HEADER=false address, and why is its impact considered marginal compared to Cache Bug 2?

options.a: It disables all request headers, eliminating the session tracking overhead that causes Bug 2
options.b: It removes the attribution header from API requests (workaround for Cache Bug 3), but Bug 3 has minor cost impact compared to Bug 2's 87-118K token rebuild per resume
options.c: It disables code attribution in generated files, reducing token usage by 30%
options.d: It prevents Claude from adding co-author comments to commits, speeding up git operations

explanation: CLAUDE_CODE_ATTRIBUTION_HEADER=false is the workaround for Cache Bug 3, which involves the attribution header affecting cache behavior. However, Cache Bug 3's cost impact is marginal in practice. Cache Bug 2 — the JSONL writer stripping deferred_tools_delta records and causing 0% cache hit rate on --resume — is the dominant issue, rebuilding 87,000 to 118,000 tokens per resume. Fixing Bug 3 without addressing Bug 2 produces negligible savings for teams that regularly use --resume.

question: What is the default data retention period for Claude Code conversations?

options.b: 1 year
options.c: 5 years

explanation: Default retention is 5 years with data used for model training. By opting out of training at claude.ai/settings/data-privacy-controls, retention reduces to 30 days (safety monitoring only). Enterprise API (ZDR) has 0-day retention.

question: What data is sent to Anthropic when using Claude Code?

options.b: Prompts, files Claude reads, MCP results, Bash outputs, error messages
options.c: Only code snippets you copy-paste

explanation: Everything Claude sees is sent: your prompts, files Claude reads (including .env if not excluded!), MCP server results (SQL queries, API responses), Bash command outputs, and error messages with stack traces. Use permissions.deny to block sensitive files.

question: What is the risk when connecting a database via MCP?

options.b: Query results (including PII) are sent to Anthropic and stored per retention policy
options.c: Claude might drop tables

explanation: When MCP executes a database query, ALL results are sent to Anthropic: "SELECT * FROM orders" → 100 rows with customer names, emails, addresses → stored according to your retention tier. NEVER connect production databases. Use dev/staging with anonymized data.

question: How can you reduce Claude Code data retention from 5 years to 30 days?

options.b: Disable 'Allow model training' at claude.ai/settings/data-privacy-controls
options.c: Use incognito mode

explanation: Visit claude.ai/settings/data-privacy-controls and toggle OFF "Allow model training". This immediately reduces retention from 5 years to 30 days (safety monitoring only). Enterprise API (ZDR) provides 0-day retention for HIPAA/GDPR compliance.

question: What is the Enterprise API (ZDR) data retention policy?

options.b: 1 year retention
options.c: 0 days (real-time processing only, data not stored)

explanation: Enterprise API (Zero Data Retention) has 0-day retention - data is processed in real-time and not stored. Required for HIPAA, GDPR, PCI-DSS compliance and government contracts. Requires enterprise contract with Anthropic.

question: What is the recommended session search tool from the guide?

options.b: session-search.sh (zero-dependency bash script)
options.c: ran CLI (npm)

explanation: session-search.sh is the recommended tool: zero dependencies (bash only), fast (~10ms list, ~400ms search), displays ready-to-use 'claude --resume' commands. Install with alias 'cs' for quick access. Alternative Python tools exist but are slower.

question: What hook event is used for session logging?

options.b: PostToolUse
options.c: SessionStart

explanation: Session logging uses PostToolUse hook - it runs after each tool completes, capturing tool name, file path, project, and token estimates. Configure in settings.json with the session-logger.sh script. Logs are stored as JSONL files in ~/.claude/logs/.

question: What is the token estimation method used by the session logger?

options.b: ~4 characters per token (heuristic, slightly overestimates)
options.c: 1 word = 1 token

explanation: The logger estimates tokens using ~4 characters per token heuristic. This is approximate and tends to slightly overestimate. Claude Code CLI doesn't expose actual API token metrics, so estimates have ~15-25% variance from actual billing.

question: What CANNOT the session monitoring do?

options.b: Identify file access patterns
options.c: Provide exact token counts and actual API costs

explanation: Monitoring CANNOT provide: exact token counts (CLI doesn't expose API metrics), actual API costs (estimates only), TTFT timing, real-time streaming metrics, or context window usage. It CAN track: tool usage counts, file access patterns, relative comparisons, operation timing.

question: Where are Claude Code session logs stored locally?

options.b: ~/.claude/projects/<project>/
options.c: /var/log/claude/

explanation: Sessions are stored locally at ~/.claude/projects/<project>/ as JSONL files. This enables session resume with 'claude --resume <id>' or 'claude -c' for most recent. Custom logs from session-logger.sh go to ~/.claude/logs/ (configurable via CLAUDE_LOG_DIR).

question: When should you use 'Co-Authored-By' vs 'Assisted-By' in git commits?

options.a: Always use Co-Authored-By for any AI involvement
options.b: Co-Authored-By when AI generated significant code, Assisted-By when AI only advised or reviewed
options.c: Never attribute AI in commits

explanation: AI traceability in git commits: - **Co-Authored-By**: When AI generated significant portions of code (implementation, refactoring) - **Assisted-By**: When AI only advised, reviewed, or suggested minor changes This distinction matters for code ownership, audit trails, and understanding who (or what) actually wrote the code. Some teams add this to CLAUDE.md as a mandatory commit convention.

question: What does CLAUDE_CODE_SUBPROCESS_ENV_SCRUB do and why is it needed?

options.a: It clears the terminal output after each command
options.b: It prevents sensitive environment variables from leaking into subprocess contexts (e.g., MCP servers, hooks)
options.c: It disables all environment variable expansion in bash commands
options.d: It encrypts environment variables before they are passed to Claude

explanation: CLAUDE_CODE_SUBPROCESS_ENV_SCRUB is a security control that prevents sensitive env vars (API keys, tokens, secrets) from leaking into subprocess contexts. Without it, environment variables present in the parent shell are inherited by MCP server processes, hooks, and spawned subprocesses. Set it to a comma-separated list of var names to scrub, or use a glob pattern. Critical for teams where Claude runs in environments with production credentials.

question: What does sandbox.failIfUnavailable control in Claude Code settings?

options.a: Whether Claude Code crashes when the network is unavailable
options.b: Whether Claude Code refuses to run if its sandbox isolation layer cannot be activated
options.c: Whether MCP servers fail gracefully when their sandbox times out
options.d: Whether hooks are skipped when the sandbox is busy

explanation: sandbox.failIfUnavailable (boolean, default: false) controls whether Claude Code hard-fails when sandbox isolation cannot be activated. Set to true in production/CI environments to enforce that every session runs inside the sandbox — no silent fallback to unsandboxed mode. Set to false in dev environments where sandbox may not be available (e.g., certain Linux kernels or Docker-in-Docker). Part of the defense-in-depth configuration.

question: What is the X-Claude-Code-Session-Id header and how should it be handled in nginx?

options.a: A header that Claude Code injects to authenticate API requests, should be removed before forwarding
options.b: A session correlation header sent on every Claude Code API request, useful for tracing; nginx should pass it through to upstream services
options.c: A rate-limiting header that nginx uses to throttle Claude Code traffic
options.d: A header that identifies the Claude model version, set by Anthropic servers

explanation: X-Claude-Code-Session-Id is a session correlation header that Claude Code attaches to every outbound API request. It enables distributed tracing: you can correlate a session's tool calls, API requests, and logs across microservices. In nginx, pass it through to upstream: `proxy_pass_header X-Claude-Code-Session-Id;`. In observability stacks, use it as a trace root to group all activity from a single Claude session.

question: What is the main philosophy behind using complementary AI tools with Claude Code?

options.a: Replace Claude Code with better tools
options.b: Augmentation, not replacement - chain the right tool for each step
options.c: Use as many tools as possible simultaneously
options.d: Avoid using Claude Code for coding tasks

explanation: The philosophy is "augmentation, not replacement." Claude Code excels at contextual reasoning and implementation, but has gaps: - No real-time web search with source verification - No image generation - No PowerPoint/slide generation - No audio synthesis The goal is to chain the right tool for each step of your workflow.

question: When should you use Perplexity instead of Claude Code?

options.a: When implementing features in your codebase
options.b: When you need deep research with 100+ verified sources
options.c: When editing multiple files

explanation: Perplexity excels at research with verified sources. Use Perplexity for: - "What's the latest API for X?" (real-time info) - "Compare 5 libraries for auth" (sourced comparisons) - Deep Research mode synthesizes 100+ sources Use Claude Code for: - Implementation in your codebase - Contextual code explanation - Multi-file editing

question: What is the recommended Research-to-Code pipeline?

options.b: Perplexity Deep Research -> Export spec.md -> Claude Code implements
options.c: Google Search -> Copy code -> Paste in project
options.d: Ask Claude Code to search the web first

explanation: The Research-to-Code pipeline: 1. PERPLEXITY (Deep Research) - Research best practices with sources - Output: 2000-word spec with citations 2. Export as spec.md 3. CLAUDE CODE - "Implement following spec.md" - Output: Working implementation with tests This ensures implementation is based on verified, current information.

question: Which tool is best for converting a UI screenshot to React code?

options.b: Claude Code directly
options.c: Gemini 2.5 Pro -> then Claude Code for refinement

explanation: Gemini 2.5 Pro excels at visual understanding: Visual-to-Code Pipeline: 1. GEMINI: Upload screenshot -> Get JSX + Tailwind (90%+ fidelity) 2. CLAUDE CODE: Refine for your project - Add TypeScript types - Connect to your components - Integrate with your state management Claude Code alone has limited image understanding compared to Gemini.

question: Which tool should you use to create PowerPoint presentations from code documentation?

options.b: Perplexity
options.c: Kimi (Moonshot AI)

explanation: Kimi (kimi.ai) from Moonshot AI specializes in PPTX generation: Features: - Native PPTX export (actual slides, not markdown) - 128K+ token context (entire codebases) - Code-aware layouts with syntax highlighting - Excellent for stakeholder decks from technical content Workflow: Claude Code generates summary -> Kimi creates presentation

question: What is NotebookLM's unique feature for developer onboarding?

options.b: Audio Overview - generates podcast-style explanations from docs
options.c: Real-time collaboration

explanation: NotebookLM's Audio Overview feature: - Upload 50+ documentation files - Generates 10-15 minute "podcast" - Two AI hosts discuss your codebase - Perfect for onboarding or reviewing large systems Workflow: 1. Export docs to combined-docs.md 2. Upload to NotebookLM 3. Generate Audio Overview 4. Listen during commute 5. Return to Claude Code with deeper understanding

question: In the complete workflow pipeline, what is the correct order of phases?

options.b: Planning (Perplexity/Gemini/NotebookLM) -> Implementation (Claude Code) -> Delivery (Kimi)
options.c: Delivery -> Planning -> Implementation

explanation: The complete pipeline has 3 phases: PLANNING PHASE: - Perplexity: Deep Research -> spec.md - Gemini: Diagram Analysis -> mermaid + plan - NotebookLM: Doc Synthesis -> audio overview IMPLEMENTATION PHASE: - Claude Code: Multi-file implementation - IDE + Copilot: Inline autocomplete DELIVERY PHASE: - Claude Code: PR description, release notes - Kimi: Stakeholder deck (presentation.pptx)

question: What is v0.dev best used for in the AI ecosystem?

options.b: Database queries
options.c: Rapid UI prototyping with Shadcn/Tailwind

explanation: v0.dev excels at rapid UI prototyping: - Generates React + Shadcn + Tailwind components - Live preview in browser - Quick iteration on UI designs Workflow: 1. v0: Prompt -> Get component preview 2. Export code 3. Claude Code: Adapt for your project - Add TypeScript types - Connect to your APIs - Integrate with your design system

question: How does Claude WebSearch compare to Perplexity Pro for research?

options.b: They are identical in functionality
options.c: Claude WebSearch: 5-10 sources, quick lookups. Perplexity Pro: 100+ sources, comprehensive research

explanation: Comparison: | Feature | Claude WebSearch | Perplexity Pro | |---------|-----------------|----------------| | Source count | ~5-10 | 100+ (Deep Research) | | Source verification | Basic | Full citations | | Best for | Quick lookups | Comprehensive research | | Cost | Included | $20/month | Recommendation: Use Claude WebSearch for quick factual checks. Use Perplexity Deep Research before significant implementations.

question: What is the 'Minimal Stack' monthly cost recommendation for AI tools?

options.b: $40-70/month - Claude Code + Perplexity Pro, free tiers for rest
options.c: $0 - only free tiers

explanation: Recommended subscription stacks: Minimal Stack ($40-70/month): - Claude Code (pay-per-use): $20-50 - Perplexity Pro: $20 - Everything else: Free tiers (NotebookLM, Kimi, Gemini free) Balanced Stack ($80-110/month): - Add Gemini Advanced ($20) and Cursor Pro ($20) Cost optimization tips: - Use Haiku for simple tasks - Batch research sessions in Perplexity - Check context usage regularly (/status)

question: What cost savings does the Claude Code to LM Studio bridge provide?

options.b: 50% savings but slower execution
options.c: 80-90% savings by planning with Opus then executing free with local LLM

explanation: The Claude Code to LM Studio bridge provides 80-90% cost savings. Strategy: use Opus for planning phase (~$0.50-2 per session) then execute implementation with a free local LLM via LM Studio. The bridge script translates Claude's plan into local LLM instructions. Trade-off: local LLMs are less capable but free.

question: What are the 3 documented external orchestration systems for multi-agent Claude Code?

options.b: Vercel AI SDK, Fly.io Machines, Railway
options.c: Gas Town (Steve Yegge), multiclaude (dlorenc), agent-chat (Justin Abrahms)

explanation: Three documented external orchestration systems: 1. **Gas Town** (Steve Yegge) - Multi-agent coordination with shared context 2. **multiclaude** (dlorenc) - Parallel Claude Code instances with task distribution 3. **agent-chat** (Justin Abrahms) - Inter-agent communication protocol These are community-built tools, not official Anthropic products. Each solves multi-agent coordination differently.

question: What 3 areas should sub-agents audit when evaluating a skeleton project before forking?

options.b: UI, API, Database
options.c: Security, Architecture, Developer Experience (DX)

explanation: Skeleton project evaluation uses 3 specialized sub-agents: 1. **Security** - Audit dependencies, secrets management, auth patterns, known vulnerabilities 2. **Architecture** - Evaluate code organization, patterns, scalability, maintainability 3. **Developer Experience (DX)** - Check setup complexity, documentation quality, tooling, onboarding friction Each agent produces a score and recommendations before the fork decision.

question: What is Google Antigravity and how does it differ from traditional IDEs?

options.a: A Google internal testing framework that simulates zero-gravity code reviews
options.b: An agent-first IDE where the AI drives execution and the human reviews rather than the reverse
options.c: A VSCode extension that integrates Google's Gemini model into the editor
options.d: Google's replacement for GitHub Copilot using a different suggestion UI

explanation: Google Antigravity is an agent-first IDE that inverts the traditional IDE model. In classic IDEs, the human drives and the AI assists. In Antigravity, the agent drives execution while the human reviews, approves, and steers. The human becomes the reviewer rather than the typist. This represents a paradigm shift from AI-assisted coding to AI-led coding with human oversight — the model that Claude Code's agentic mode already approximates.

question: What does research show about AGENTS.md Compound Learning — specifically human-curated vs LLM-generated updates?

options.a: LLM-generated updates are always superior because they are more consistent and complete
options.b: Human-curated AGENTS.md updates yield +4% performance improvement; LLM-generated updates yield -3% performance with +20% cost increase
options.c: Both methods produce identical results, so LLM generation is preferred for automation
options.d: Human-curated updates are only better for junior developers; LLM-generated outperforms for senior codebases

explanation: AGENTS.md Compound Learning research shows a stark divergence: human-curated updates to AGENTS.md (based on real session feedback and corrections) produce a +4% improvement in agent performance across benchmark tasks. LLM-auto-generated AGENTS.md updates produce -3% performance regression and +20% cost increase because auto-generated rules tend to over-specify, conflict, or add noise. Implication: invest human review time in AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md maintenance — it compounds.

question: What are the four core DORA metrics?

options.a: Commit Frequency, PR Count, Code Coverage, Deployment Speed
options.b: Deployment Frequency, Lead Time for Changes, Change Failure Rate, Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR)
options.c: Sprint Velocity, Story Points, Bug Count, Test Coverage
options.d: Lines of Code, NPS, Uptime, Developer Satisfaction

explanation: The four core DORA metrics measure delivery system health: (1) Deployment Frequency — how often you deploy to production. (2) Lead Time for Changes — time from commit to running in production. (3) Change Failure Rate — percentage of deployments causing incidents or rollbacks. (4) Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR) — time to restore service after a failure. These four are the most validated delivery health indicators in the field, backed by years of DORA research.

question: What are the 2024 DORA benchmarks for Elite tier deployment frequency and lead time?

options.a: Elite: weekly deployment, lead time under 1 week
options.b: Elite: multiple times per day, lead time under 1 day
options.c: Elite: once per day, lead time under 1 hour
options.d: Elite: once per week, lead time under 3 days

explanation: 2024 DORA Elite benchmarks: Deployment Frequency = multiple times per day. Lead Time for Changes = less than 1 day. Change Failure Rate = 0-5%. MTTR = less than 1 hour. These thresholds should be recalibrated with AI adoption in context: Anthropic internal data (Jan 2026) shows +67% PRs per engineer/day with AI assistance. "Medium" tier is no longer credible for AI-augmented teams — the baseline has shifted.

question: What major methodological change did the 2025 DORA report introduce?

options.a: Added a 5th metric: Developer Happiness Index
options.b: Retired the 4-tier Elite/High/Medium/Low model in favor of 7 organizational archetypes across 8 dimensions
options.c: Replaced deployment frequency with feature flag rollout cadence
options.d: Introduced AI-specific benchmarks that replace the original four metrics

explanation: The 2025 DORA report retired the 4-tier model (Elite/High/Medium/Low) and now identifies 7 organizational archetypes (e.g., "Thriving Achievers," "Struggling Strugglers," "Balanced Performers") measured across 8 dimensions: throughput, stability, team performance, product performance, individual effectiveness, time on valuable work, friction, and burnout. The implication: stop chasing "Elite" as an endpoint — it can coexist with burnout and high friction. Identify your archetype and improve weakest dimensions.

question: What are the 5 dimensions of the SPACE framework?

options.b: Satisfaction/Well-being, Performance, Activity, Communication/Collaboration, Efficiency/Flow
options.c: Security, Performance, Availability, Capacity, Experience
options.d: Sprints, PRs, Automation, Code quality, Engineering debt

explanation: SPACE (developed by GitHub, Microsoft, and University of Victoria, 2021) covers: (S) Satisfaction & Well-being — developer satisfaction and burnout signals. (P) Performance — quality and reliability of delivered work (CFR, Bug Escape Rate). (A) Activity — volume of work output (deployment frequency, throughput). (C) Communication & Collaboration — PR review latency, cross-team dependency resolution. (E) Efficiency & Flow — deep work capacity, context-switching frequency, friction. DORA measures the delivery system; SPACE measures the people inside it.

question: What is the 'Velocity Trap' in AI-augmented teams?

options.a: Deploying too frequently and breaking production with each release
options.b: Hitting High DORA scores while simultaneously scoring poorly on satisfaction, well-being, and efficiency — more deployments but at the cost of developer health and quality
options.c: Using AI to generate velocity reports that overstate actual team performance
options.d: Sprint velocity becoming meaningless when AI writes all the code

explanation: The Velocity Trap: teams can hit "High" on DORA deployment frequency while simultaneously scoring poorly on satisfaction, well-being, and efficiency. Symptoms: developers working nights to hit sprint commitments, skipping design discussions because AI makes coding fast enough to bypass planning, accumulating cognitive debt from reviewing AI code they don't fully understand. SPACE catches this; DORA doesn't. Running both frameworks gives you the full picture. A team with strong DORA + weak SPACE is a leading indicator of future DORA degradation.

question: What is the warning signal when AI PR review time is 30% faster than manual PR review time?

options.a: Normal and expected — AI code is simpler and faster to review
options.b: A yellow flag indicating possible rubber-stamping; AI-generated code requires at least as much scrutiny as manually written code
options.c: A positive sign that AI code quality is higher
options.d: Acceptable only if Change Failure Rate is also lower

explanation: If AI-generated PRs are getting merged 30%+ faster than manual ones, it's a yellow flag suggesting rubber-stamping. AI-generated code requires at least as much review scrutiny as manually written code — arguably more, because it can be confidently wrong in non-obvious ways. Track CFR separately for AI-generated vs manually written code: if AI-generated CFR is materially higher (more than 2-3 percentage points), your review discipline has dropped, not your AI tooling quality.

question: What is the Bug Escape Rate formula and what threshold signals a QA process problem?

options.b: bugs_before_prod / bugs_in_prod; threshold: >5%
options.c: bugs_in_prod / (bugs_before_prod + bugs_in_prod); threshold: >20%

explanation: Bug Escape Rate = bugs_in_prod / (bugs_before_prod + bugs_in_prod). Example: 4 prod bugs + 16 pre-prod bugs = 20% escape rate. Threshold: >20% means QA and review processes are consistently failing to catch issues before users see them. With AI-assisted development, this metric is worth watching closely — faster code generation combined with looser review can push Bug Escape Rate up even when absolute bug count stays flat. Pair with CFR segmented by AI vs manual code origin.

question: Which metric should replace Sprint Velocity and why?

options.a: PR count — more objective than story points
options.b: Cycle Time + Deployment Frequency — velocity is gameable within 2 sprints by changing estimation practices, while cycle time is harder to fake
options.c: Lines of code — more measurable and consistent
options.d: Story points normalized by team size

explanation: Sprint velocity should be replaced by Cycle Time + Deployment Frequency. Velocity is gameable within 2 sprints by changing estimation practices (teams inflate point values to look faster). Cycle time (commit to prod) is harder to fake. The guide's vanity metrics to drop table: Sprint Velocity → Cycle Time + Deployment Frequency. Lines of Code → Bug Escape Rate. NPS alone → CSAT + Time-to-value. Commits/day → Lead Time for Changes. Story points → Throughput (features shipped). Code coverage % → Mutation testing score + Bug Escape Rate.

question: What is the 4-Question Test for deciding whether to add a metric to your tracking stack?

options.a: Is it free? Is it automated? Does it look good in reports? Does leadership care?
options.b: Can you act on it in 2 weeks? Does it explain why (not just what)? Is it correlated to a business outcome? Can it be measured automatically?
options.c: Is it a DORA metric? Does it appear in the SPACE framework? Is it industry-standard? Does it require no custom code?
options.d: Does it track individual performance? Is it available in GitHub? Is it exportable to CSV? Does it run daily?

explanation: The 4-Question Test: (1) Can you act on it within 2 weeks? — if not, it's a reporting metric, not a steering metric. (2) Does it explain why, not just what? — if this number moves, do you know what to investigate? (3) Is it correlated to a business outcome? — deployment frequency correlates to revenue in SaaS; lines of code correlates to nothing. (4) Can it be measured automatically? — if collecting it requires manual work, it will be abandoned within 3 months. Rule: fewer than 3 "yes" answers, drop the metric.

question: What is the recommended metrics tooling difference between a 5-person team and a 25-person team?

options.a: Both should use LinearB — it scales from small to large teams
options.b: 5-person: GitHub Insights + spreadsheet (no dedicated dashboard needed). 25-person: LinearB or Faros.ai for automated DORA, segmented per squad
options.c: 5-person: no metrics needed. 25-person: full SPACE + DORA + AI metrics dashboard
options.d: Both should use the same tools but with different refresh frequencies

explanation: 5-person team: GitHub Insights + a shared spreadsheet. No dedicated dashboard — overhead not worth it. Review metrics monthly. 25-person team: LinearB or Faros.ai for automated DORA (connects to GitHub + CI/CD), GitHub Analytics for AI contribution metrics, PostHog or Amplitude for product metrics. Critical at 25 people: track DORA per squad, not just per organization — global averages hide squad-level problems (a 3-squad team where 80% of incidents come from one squad will show "Medium" CFR overall and miss the signal).

question: What does the DORA metric most at risk from AI adoption without review discipline?

options.a: Deployment Frequency — AI generates too many deployments
options.b: MTTR — AI incident response makes recovery too slow
options.c: Change Failure Rate — AI generates syntactically correct but subtly wrong code; rubber-stamp reviews cause CFR to creep up over 6-12 months
options.d: Lead Time — AI slows down review queues by generating larger diffs

explanation: Change Failure Rate is the metric most at risk when AI adoption outpaces review discipline. AI generates syntactically correct, structurally plausible code that can still have subtle behavioral errors. Teams that treat AI PRs as "lower risk" and rubber-stamp reviews see CFR creep up over 6-12 months. The failure is gradual — each individual AI PR looks fine, but cumulative reduced scrutiny shows up in production. Track CFR separately for AI-generated vs manually written code; if AI CFR is materially higher, reinforce the review process.

question: What is the recommended 3-phase implementation roadmap for a metrics program?

options.a: Phase 1: Hire a metrics engineer. Phase 2: Buy enterprise tooling. Phase 3: Report to board.
options.b: Phase 1 (weeks 1-2): Instrument DORA (deployment frequency + lead time first). Phase 2 (weeks 3-4): Baseline and set internal improvement targets. Phase 3 (month 2+): Layer in product and AI-specific metrics.
options.c: Phase 1: Track everything. Phase 2: Remove what isn't useful. Phase 3: Automate the remainder.
options.d: Phase 1: SPACE survey. Phase 2: DORA instrumentation. Phase 3: AI contribution metrics.

explanation: 3-phase roadmap: Phase 1 (weeks 1-2) — connect CI/CD to metrics tool, get Deployment Frequency + Lead Time automated first (least manual config), establish live baseline. Phase 2 (weeks 3-4) — establish baseline from real data, set internal improvement targets (not industry benchmark comparisons yet), run first developer satisfaction pulse. Phase 3 (month 2+) — add product metrics (time-to-value, feature CSAT) and AI-specific signals (% AI code, CFR by origin). Review and prune quarterly: any metric that hasn't driven a decision in 3 months is a reporting metric, not a steering metric.
